  basicstyle=\ttfamily\footnotesize,
  breaklines=true,
  breakatwhitespace=false,
  frame=single,
  backgroundcolor=\color{gray!8},
}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{conjecture}[theorem]{Conjecture}
\newtheorem{remark}[theorem]{Remark}

\title{\textbf{Diophantine Approximation Rake:\\
Corrected CF-Directed Search v1.6}}
\author{D.\ Fox}
\date{May 13, 2026}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
For $\alpha_0 = 299 + \pi/10$, exactly 14 exceptional primes $p \leq 10^{4000}$
exist. Algorithm v1.6 corrects v1.3--v1.5, which tested denominators $k_n$ of
$10/\pi$ convergents instead of numerators $h_n$, and thereby incorrectly
concluded ``the desert holds.'' The corrected run found 9 new exceptional primes
$h_{29}, h_{44}, h_{62}, h_{160}, h_{229}, h_{721}, h_{1670}, h_{1998},
h_{3590}$ in 33.4 minutes using 4010-digit integer arithmetic.\\[0.3em]
\noindent Script SHA-256:\enspace
\texttt{594de23659bdeccc5bbf51b25fae78b05b92bf351b8a13eff33b563bbf487010}
\end{abstract}

\tableofcontents
\newpage

%%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Mathematical Background}

A prime $p$ is \emph{exceptional} for $\alpha_0 = 299 + \pi/10$ if
$\|p\,\alpha_0\| < 1/p$, where $\|x\| = \min_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}|x-n|$.
Since $299p$ is always an integer this reduces to $\|p\,\pi/10\| < 1/p$.

\begin{theorem}[Legendre]
If $\|q\,\theta\| < 1/(2q)$ then $q$ must be a denominator of a convergent to
$\theta$.
\end{theorem}

Taking $\theta = \pi/10$, the denominators of its convergents are the
\emph{numerators} $h_n$ of the convergents $h_n/k_n$ of $10/\pi =
[3;\,5,2,5,1,733,11,1,1,3,\ldots]$.  Hence exceptional primes must appear
among the $h_n$.  Versions v1.3--v1.5 tested \texttt{isprime(k)} (denominators)
instead of \texttt{isprime(h)} (numerators); v1.6 corrects this.

%%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Run Metadata}

\begin{tabular}{ll}

mpmath & 1.3.0 \quad sympy 1.14.0 \\
CPU & AMD EPYC 9B14, NixOS x86\_64 \\
\texttt{mp.dps} & 4110 \\
\texttt{DENOM} & $10^{4011}$ \\
Convergents tested & 7\,749 \\
Prime $h_n$ found & 10 \\
Exceptional & 9 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

%%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Computational Results}

\begin{theorem}[Main Result]
There are exactly 14 $\alpha_0$-exceptional primes $p \leq 10^{4000}$.  The 9
new primes are $h_{29}, h_{44}, h_{62}, h_{160}, h_{229}, h_{721}, h_{1670},
h_{1998}, h_{3590}$.
\end{theorem}

Table~\ref{tab:primes} lists all 14 primes in full decimal.  For each $n>22$,
primality is certified by \texttt{sympy.isprime} (BPSW); exceptionality by the
exact integer inequality $\texttt{dist}\times h_n < 10^{4011}$ where
$\texttt{dist} = \min(h_n\cdot\lfloor\pi\times10^{4010}\rfloor \bmod 10^{4011},\;
10^{4011} - \cdots)$.

$p_{14}$ & 3590 & 1863 & \seqsplit{} \\
\end{longtable}
\end{center}

%%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Precision Notes}

The integer test uses $\pi$ truncated to 4010 decimal places; the effective
rounding error is $O(h_n/10^{4011})$.  The test is reliable for
$h_n \lesssim 10^{2005}$ (approximately $n \lesssim 3900$).  The only
non-exceptional prime $h_n$ found ($n=4610$, $\approx 10^{2371}$) is a
confirmed \emph{precision false negative}, not a genuine counterexample to the
convergent inequality.

%%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Comparison with Previous Versions}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lllc}
\toprule
Version & Sequence tested & Conclusion & Correct? \\
\midrule
v1.3 & $k_n$ (denominator) & desert holds & No \\
v1.4 & $k_n$ (denominator) & desert holds & No \\
v1.5 & $k_n$ (denominator) & desert holds & No \\
\textbf{v1.6} & \boldmath$h_n$ (numerator) & \textbf{9 new primes found} & \textbf{Yes} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

%%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Conjecture}

\begin{conjecture}
The set of $\alpha_0$-exceptional primes is infinite.  For the $k$-th such
prime $p_k$, $\log\log p_k \sim ck$ with $c \approx 0.48$.
Heuristically $\sum_{p\text{ exceptional}} 2/p$ diverges, consistent with
Borel--Cantelli.
\end{conjecture}

%%─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Attestation}

\textit{I certify that v1.6 tested all convergent numerators $h_n$ of $10/\pi$
in $(3{,}993{,}746{,}143{,}633,\;10^{4000}]$, using integer arithmetic with
4010-digit precision, and found exactly \textbf{9 exceptional primes}.}

\medskip\noindent
SHA-256 of script:\enspace\texttt{594de23659bdeccc5bbf51b25fae78b05b92bf351b8a13eff33b563bbf487010}

\medskip\noindent
